purpose: Redo of the elements batch after the 'spec-sheet trap' feedback: deliberately span orthogonal dimension CATEGORIES (discovery history, etymology, human use, economics, biology, health, culture, astrophysics), with only ~3 intrinsic-physical-property questions and those limited to the reflex-defeaters that landed. No iconic-use hints; per-topic warhorses (mercury-liquid, nitrogen-air, diamond-hardest) cut. NOT filed to the library.
category_spread: history-of-science, archaeology, etymology, naming, biology, health, economic-history, culture, market-price, physical-property x3, astrophysics — no super-category > ~1/3 of set

candidates.q: Which element was first found in the light of the Sun, years before anyone found it on Earth?
candidates.note: Spotted in the Sun's spectrum in 1868, named for Helios; not isolated on Earth until 1895. Category: history of science. Finalists Helium, Hydrogen, Neon.

candidates.q: Which metal did humans learn to work and use first, many thousands of years ago?
candidates.note: Worked from ~9000 BC, before the Bronze and Iron Ages. Flag: gold was also worked very early. Finalists Copper, Gold, Iron, Tin.

candidates.q: Which metal is named after a mischievous goblin from old German folklore?
candidates.note: From 'kobold'; miners blamed the goblin when the troublesome ore poisoned them and yielded no copper. Category: etymology. Finalists Cobalt, Nickel, Bismuth.

candidates.q: Marie Curie named which element in honour of the country she was born in?
candidates.note: Named for Poland, 1898. Category: history/naming. Finalists Polonium, Radium.

candidates.q: Which metal sits at the centre of your blood's red cells, carrying oxygen around your body?
candidates.note: In haemoglobin; too little causes anaemia. Category: biology. Finalists Iron, Copper, Zinc.

candidates.q: A diet missing which element makes the thyroid swell into the lump called a goitre?
candidates.note: Why table salt is iodized. Category: health. Finalists Iodine, Iron, Calcium, Fluorine.

candidates.q: Which everyday metal, common as dirt today, was once so hard to purify that it was prized above gold?
candidates.note: Napoleon III reputedly served top guests on aluminium; the Washington Monument was capped with it. Cheap only after electrolysis (1886). Category: economic history. Finalists Aluminium, Silver, Platinum.

candidates.q: Poisoning by which toxic metal, used to make felt hats, is the origin of the phrase 'mad as a hatter'?
candidates.note: Mercury compounds in hat-making damaged hatters' nervous systems. Category: cultural history. Finalists Mercury, Lead, Arsenic.

candidates.q: Which metal is usually the most expensive of all the precious metals?
candidates.note: Kept from batch 1 — rated 'interesting'. Beats gold/platinum; ~100x rarer than gold; very volatile. Finalists Rhodium, Gold, Platinum, Palladium.

candidates.q: Which metal carries electricity better than any other?
candidates.note: Kept — rated well ('it's not gold?'). Edges copper. Finalists Silver, Copper, Gold, Aluminium.

candidates.q: Which metal is the most reactive of all — set it on water and it bursts into flame at once?
candidates.note: Reveal should say HOW it's ranked: reactivity of the alkali metals rises down the group (Na < K < Rb < Cs) as the outer electron is held more loosely; francium would be higher but is too rare to count. Finalists Caesium, Rubidium, Potassium, Sodium.

candidates.q: Stars, including our Sun, are made mostly of which element — the fuel they burn?
candidates.note: ~73% of the Sun's mass; fused into helium. Category: astrophysics. Finalists Hydrogen, Helium, Carbon.
